questions.q: An ideal $\mathfrak{p}\subset A$ is prime exactly when:
choices: $\mathfrak{p}$ is maximal among ideals not containing $1$
$\mathfrak{p}$ is generated by a single element
questions.hint: $\mathfrak{p}$ is prime iff the quotient $A/\mathfrak{p}$ has no zero divisors, i.e.
questions.explain: $\mathfrak{p}$ is prime iff the quotient $A/\mathfrak{p}$ has no zero divisors, i.e. is an integral domain. Maximal corresponds to the stronger condition $A/\mathfrak{p}$ a field.

questions.q: How many ideals does $\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z}$ have?
questions.hint: Ideals of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ correspond to divisors of $n$.
questions.explain: Ideals of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ correspond to divisors of $n$. Divisors of $12$: $1,2,3,4,6,12$, so $6$ ideals.

questions.q: Which ideal of $\mathbb{Z}$ has zero set $V(I) = \{(2),(3),(5)\}\subset\mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z})$?
questions.hint: $V(I)=\{\mathfrak{p}:I\subseteq\mathfrak{p}\}$.
questions.explain: $V(I)=\{\mathfrak{p}:I\subseteq\mathfrak{p}\}$. In $\mathbb{Z}$, $(30)\subseteq (p)$ iff $p\in\{2,3,5\}$, so $V((30))=\{(2),(3),(5)\}$.

hard.q: Let $A=k[x,y]$ and $I=(x^2,xy,y^2)$. Which is the correct chain of inclusions relating $I$, $\sqrt{I}$, and the maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m}=(x,y)$?
hard.explain: $I\subsetneq\mathfrak{m}=(x,y)$ since $x\notin I$. But $x^2,y^2\in I$ means $x,y\in\sqrt{I}$, so $\sqrt{I}=\mathfrak{m}$.

hard.q: How many elements does $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(2,x^2+x+1)$ have?
hard.hint: Reduce mod $2$ first and check irreducibility.
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(2,x^2+x+1)\cong\mathbb{F}_2[x]/(x^2+x+1)$. Since $x^2+x+1$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_2$, this is the field $\mathbb{F}_4$ with $4$ elements.

questions.q: In the polynomial ring $k[x]$ over a field $k$, which of the following is a maximal ideal?
questions.hint: In a PID, maximal ideals are $(f)$ with $f$ irreducible.
questions.explain: In a PID, maximal ideals are $(f)$ with $f$ irreducible. $x^2+1$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{R}$; $x^2-1=(x-1)(x+1)$ is not. $(0)$ is prime but not maximal.

questions.q: How many maximal ideals does $\mathbb{Z}/30\mathbb{Z}$ have?
questions.hint: In an Artinian ring every prime is maximal.
questions.explain: In an Artinian ring every prime is maximal. Primes of $\mathbb{Z}/30$ correspond to primes dividing $30=2\cdot 3\cdot 5$, giving $(2),(3),(5)$: $3$ maximal ideals.

questions.q: Select all primes of $\mathbb{Z}[x]$ that are maximal.
questions.hint: $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]=2$, so maximal ideals have height $2$.
questions.explain: $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]=2$, so maximal ideals have height $2$. $(0)$ is generic (height $0$); $(2),(x)$ are height $1$ (quotient is a domain but not a field). $(2,x)$ and $(3,x^2+1)$ are height-$2$ with residue field $\mathbb{F}_2$ and $\mathbb{F}_9$ respectively.

hard.q: Which is true of every commutative ring $A$ with $1\ne 0$?
choices: $A$ has at least one prime ideal, and every maximal ideal is prime
$A$ has only finitely many prime ideals
Every prime ideal of $A$ is maximal
The intersection of all maximal ideals is $(0)$
hard.hint: One of these is Krull's theorem (Zorn); others fail for $\mathbb{Z}$, $k[x]$, or $k[[x]]$.
hard.explain: Krull's theorem (via Zorn) guarantees every proper ideal sits inside a maximal one, so maximals exist, and maximal $\Rightarrow$ prime (quotient by maximal is a field, hence a domain). $\mathbb{Z}$ has infinitely many primes; $(0)\subsetneq(2)$ in $\mathbb{Z}$ shows primes need not be maximal; $\bigcap\mathfrak{m}$ can be nonzero in a local ring.

hard.q: In $A = \mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$, the prime $(2)\subset\mathbb{Z}$ factors as $(2, 1+\sqrt{-5})^2$. How many primes of $A$ lie over $(2)$?
hard.explain: $(2)$ is ramified: $(2)A = \mathfrak{p}^2$ with $\mathfrak{p} = (2, 1+\sqrt{-5})$, so exactly one prime lies above $(2)$. Note $A/\mathfrak{p}\cong\mathbb{F}_2$.

hard.q: For $A = k[x,y]$ with $k$ algebraically closed, Hilbert's Nullstellensatz says every maximal ideal has the form:
hard.explain: By the weak Nullstellensatz, maximal ideals of $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ (algebraically closed $k$) biject with points $(a_1,\dots,a_n)\in k^n$ via $(x_1-a_1,\dots,x_n-a_n)$. $(f)$ is only a prime of height $1$ (a curve), not maximal.

questions.q: In $\mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z})$, the closure $\overline{\{(0)\}}$ equals:
questions.hint: $\overline{\{\mathfrak{p}\}} = V(\mathfrak{p}) = \{\mathfrak{q}:\mathfrak{p}\subseteq\mathfrak{q}\}$.
questions.explain: $\overline{\{\mathfrak{p}\}} = V(\mathfrak{p}) = \{\mathfrak{q}:\mathfrak{p}\subseteq\mathfrak{q}\}$. Since $(0)\subseteq\mathfrak{q}$ for all primes, $\overline{\{(0)\}}=\mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z})$: the generic point is dense.

questions.q: How many points does the distinguished open set $D(6) = \{\mathfrak{p}:6\notin\mathfrak{p}\} \subset \mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z}/30)$ contain?
questions.hint: Primes of $\mathbb{Z}/30$ are $(2),(3),(5)$.
questions.explain: Primes of $\mathbb{Z}/30$ are $(2),(3),(5)$. $D(6)$ excludes primes containing $6$, i.e. containing $2$ or $3$, so it excludes $(2)$ and $(3)$, leaving $\{(5)\}$: $1$ point.

questions.q: Which subset of $\mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z})$ is NOT closed in the Zariski topology?
questions.hint: Closed sets are $V(I)$ for ideals $I$.
questions.explain: Closed sets are $V(I)$ for ideals $I$. $\{(2),(3)\}=V((6))$ is closed; $\mathrm{Spec}=V((0))$; $\emptyset=V((1))$. But $\{(0)\}$ would be $V(I)$ only if $I=(0)$, and $V((0))=\mathrm{Spec}$, not $\{(0)\}$. The generic point $(0)$ is not closed.

hard.q: For a ring $A$, the map $\mathrm{Spec}(A/I)\to\mathrm{Spec}(A)$ induced by $A\twoheadrightarrow A/I$ is:
hard.hint: Primes of $A/I$ correspond to primes of $A$ containing $I$.
hard.explain: Primes of $A/I$ biject with primes of $A$ containing $I$, i.e. with $V(I)$; the bijection is a Zariski homeomorphism. Similarly $\mathrm{Spec}(S^{-1}A)$ embeds as the open set of primes disjoint from $S$.

hard.q: Select all properties that $\mathrm{Spec}(A)$ always satisfies as a topological space.
choices: quasi-compact (every open cover has a finite subcover)
hard.explain: $\mathrm{Spec}(A)$ is always quasi-compact and $T_0$, never Hausdorff unless zero-dimensional (non-closed generic points). Connectedness fails for $A\times B$: $\mathrm{Spec}(A\times B)=\mathrm{Spec}(A)\sqcup\mathrm{Spec}(B)$.

hard.q: How many irreducible components does $\mathrm{Spec}(k[x,y]/(xy(x-1)))$ have?
hard.explain: $(xy(x-1)) = (x)\cap(y)\cap(x-1)$ as a radical ideal. The three minimal primes correspond to three irreducible components: the $y$-axis $(x)$, the $x$-axis $(y)$, and the line $x=1$.

questions.q: The nilradical $\mathrm{nil}(A)$ of a commutative ring $A$ equals:
questions.hint: $\mathrm{nil}(A) = \{x : x^n = 0\text{ some }n\}$.
questions.explain: $\mathrm{nil}(A) = \{x : x^n = 0\text{ some }n\}$. A classical theorem: this equals $\bigcap\mathfrak{p}$ over all primes. The intersection over minimal primes also works (it's the same set).

questions.q: What is the nilradical of $\mathbb{Z}/72\mathbb{Z}$? (Give its smallest nonnegative generator.)
questions.explain: $72 = 2^3\cdot 3^2$. Nilradical = $(\mathrm{rad}(72)) = (2\cdot 3) = (6)$. An element is nilpotent iff it's divisible by every prime dividing $72$, i.e. by $6$.

questions.q: An element $x\in A$ lies in the Jacobson radical $\mathrm{Jac}(A)$ iff:
choices: $1-xy$ is a unit for every $y\in A$
questions.hint: Characterization: $x\in\mathrm{Jac}(A) \Leftrightarrow 1-xy \in A^\times$ for all $y$.
questions.explain: Characterization: $x\in\mathrm{Jac}(A) \Leftrightarrow 1-xy \in A^\times$ for all $y$. In a local ring this says $x$ is in the maximal ideal; more generally $\mathrm{Jac}(A) = \bigcap\mathfrak{m}$.

hard.q: Which inclusion between nilradical and Jacobson radical is always true for commutative rings $A$?
hard.explain: Every maximal ideal is prime, so $\bigcap\mathfrak{m} \supseteq \bigcap\mathfrak{p}$, i.e. $\mathrm{Jac}(A)\supseteq\mathrm{nil}(A)$. Equality fails e.g. for $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$: $\mathrm{nil}=(0)$ but $\mathrm{Jac}=(p)\ne 0$.

hard.q: Select all rings $A$ for which $\mathrm{nil}(A) = \mathrm{Jac}(A)$.
hard.explain: In Artinian rings, every prime is maximal, so the two intersections agree. Jacobson rings are defined by $\mathrm{nil}(A/I)=\mathrm{Jac}(A/I)$ for every $I$, in particular for $I=0$. For $\mathbb{Z}$: $\mathrm{nil}=(0)$, $\mathrm{Jac}=\bigcap(p)=(0)$ — equal! But $k[x]$: $\mathrm{nil}=(0)$, $\mathrm{Jac}=\bigcap(f)=(0)$... actually these are both $(0)$ for one-dimensional Jacobson domains. Option B is valid too; but stricter rings like $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$ fail.

hard.q: Let $A=k[x,y]/(x^2,xy)$. Compute $\dim_k(\mathrm{nil}(A))$ as a $k$-vector space.
hard.hint: $A/(\bar x)\cong k[y]$ is reduced, so $\mathrm{nil}(A) \subseteq (\bar x)$.
hard.explain: $A$ has basis $\{1,\bar y,\bar y^2,\dots,\bar x\}$ over $k$. Since $A/(\bar x)\cong k[y]$ is reduced, $\mathrm{nil}(A) = (\bar x)$. And $(\bar x) = k\cdot\bar x$ (since $\bar x\bar y = 0$), giving $\dim_k\mathrm{nil}(A)=1$.

questions.q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is $\mathbb{Z}/4 \otimes_\mathbb{Z} \mathbb{Z}/6$?
questions.hint: $\mathbb{Z}/m\otimes_\mathbb{Z}\mathbb{Z}/n \cong \mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$.
questions.explain: $\mathbb{Z}/m\otimes_\mathbb{Z}\mathbb{Z}/n \cong \mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(4,6)=2$.

questions.q: How many $\mathbb{Z}$-submodules does $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ have?
questions.hint: Submodules of $\mathbb{Z}/6$ correspond to divisors of $6$: $1,2,3,6$, giving $4$ submodules: $0,\;2\mathbb{Z}/6,\;3\mathbb{Z}/6,\;\mathbb{Z}/6$ itself.
questions.explain: Submodules of $\mathbb{Z}/6$ correspond to divisors of $6$: $1,2,3,6$, giving $4$ submodules: $0,\;2\mathbb{Z}/6,\;3\mathbb{Z}/6,\;\mathbb{Z}/6$ itself.

questions.q: For which sequence is the claim 'tensoring with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{Z}$ preserves exactness' FALSE?
questions.hint: Tensoring the first sequence with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2 \xrightarrow{2=0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, so injectivity fails.
questions.explain: Tensoring the first sequence with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2 \xrightarrow{2=0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, so injectivity fails. The other sequences either split or are tensored with a coprime factor.

hard.q: Let $M, N$ be finitely generated modules over a commutative ring $A$. The canonical map $M^\vee \otimes N \to \mathrm{Hom}(M, N)$ (where $M^\vee = \mathrm{Hom}(M, A)$) is an isomorphism when:
hard.explain: $M^\vee\otimes N\to\mathrm{Hom}(M,N)$ is an iso when $M$ is f.g. projective (dualizable in $A\mathbf{-Mod}$). It fails for general f.g. $M$ (e.g. torsion modules over a domain).

hard.q: Select all statements that hold for modules $M$ over a commutative ring $A$.
choices: Direct sum is a left adjoint to the diagonal functor
Every finitely generated module over a PID is free
hard.explain: $\mathrm{Hom}(A,-)$ and $-\otimes A$ are both the identity functor on $A\mathbf{-Mod}$. Coproducts (direct sums) are left adjoint to diagonal. The last is false: $\mathbb{Z}/n$ is f.g. over $\mathbb{Z}$ but not free.

hard.q: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_p}(\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^3) \otimes_{\mathbb{F}_p[x]} \mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^7))$.
hard.explain: $\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^3)\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_p[x]}\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^7) \cong \mathbb{F}_p[x]/((x^3)+(x^7))=\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^3)$ since $(x^3)\supseteq(x^7)$. Dimension $= 3$.

questions.q: A commutative ring $A$ is Noetherian iff:
choices: every ascending chain of ideals $I_1\subseteq I_2\subseteq\cdots$ stabilizes
$A$ has only finitely many prime ideals
questions.hint: The ascending chain condition is the defining property; equivalently, every ideal is finitely generated.
questions.explain: The ascending chain condition is the defining property; equivalently, every ideal is finitely generated. Principal ideal domains are stronger; finite prime count fails for $\mathbb{Z}$ (which is Noetherian).

questions.q: Which of the following are Noetherian?
questions.hint: $\mathbb{Z}$ is a PID (Noetherian).
questions.explain: $\mathbb{Z}$ is a PID (Noetherian). $k[x,y]/(xy)$ is a quotient of the Noetherian $k[x,y]$. $k[x_1,x_2,\dots]$ has the non-stabilizing chain $(x_1)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2)\subsetneq\cdots$. $C(\mathbb{R})$ has chains of vanishing ideals that don't stabilize.

questions.q: The Hilbert basis theorem says: if $A$ is Noetherian then so is $A[x]$. Applying this repeatedly, $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ is Noetherian over any field $k$. How many generators does the ideal $(x^5, x^3 y, y^7)\subset k[x,y]$ need, minimally?
questions.hint: In a monomial ideal, minimal generators are the monomials not divisible by other monomials in the generating set.
questions.explain: In a monomial ideal, minimal generators are the monomials not divisible by other monomials in the generating set. $x^5, x^3y, y^7$: none divides another, so all three are needed. $3$ generators.

hard.q: Arrange the steps of the proof of the Hilbert basis theorem (that $A$ Noetherian $\Rightarrow A[x]$ Noetherian).
items: Let $I\subseteq A[x]$ be an ideal and form the ideal $J\subseteq A$ of leading coefficients
Choose finitely many $f_1,\dots,f_r\in I$ whose leading coefficients generate $J$
For any $g\in I$, subtract a combination $\sum a_i x^{k_i} f_i$ to reduce $\deg g$
Reduce to a finite-dimensional sub-case and invoke Noetherianness of $A$
hard.hint: Start by extracting data from $I$; end by invoking ACC on $A$.
hard.explain: The classical proof: first define $J$ = leading coefficients of elements of $I$ (this is an ideal of $A$), pick generators via Noetherianness of $A$, use them to reduce any $g\in I$ modulo bounded-degree pieces, and finish with f.g. coefficient modules.

hard.q: Which ring is NOT Noetherian but has an 'ascending chain condition on finitely generated ideals'?
choices: A polynomial ring in infinitely many variables
A valuation ring of rank $1$ with non-discrete value group (e.g. $\mathbb{Z}[\frac{1}{2},\frac{1}{3},\ldots]_{(p)}$-type constructions)
hard.hint: A valuation ring has totally ordered ideals — but the ordering might not be well-founded.
hard.explain: Non-discrete valuation rings have totally ordered ideal structure, so principal/f.g. ideals form a chain, but the order-type can be $\mathbb{R}$-like, violating ACC on all ideals. Polynomial rings in $\infty$ variables fail ACC on f.g. ideals too.

hard.q: Let $A = k[x,y,z]/(x^2, xy, yz, z^2)$ (monomial quotient). How many associated primes does $A$ have as an $A$-module of itself?
hard.explain: The only minimal prime containing $(x^2,xy,yz,z^2)$ is the nilradical's associated prime $(x,y,z)$: every nonzero element's annihilator is contained in $(x,y,z)$. One associated prime.

questions.q: Akizuki–Hopkins says a commutative ring is Artinian iff it is:
choices: Noetherian and zero-dimensional (every prime is maximal)
a local ring with finitely generated maximal ideal
questions.hint: Artinian = DCC on ideals = Noetherian + Krull dimension $0$.
questions.explain: Artinian = DCC on ideals = Noetherian + Krull dimension $0$. Reduced Artinian rings are products of fields, but the unreduced cases like $k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ are also Artinian and are the deformation-theoretic point.

questions.q: What is the length $\ell_k(A)$ (equivalently $\dim_k A$) of $A = k[x,y]/(x,y)^3$ as a $k$-vector space?
questions.explain: Basis $\{1, x, y, x^2, xy, y^2\}$. Length grows as $1 + 2 + 3 = 6 = \binom{3+1}{2}$. In general $\dim_k k[x_1,\dots,x_d]/(x_1,\dots,x_d)^n = \binom{n+d-1}{d}$.

questions.q: Select all rings that are Artinian local with residue field $k$ (so live in $\mathrm{Art}_k$).
questions.hint: $k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$, $k[x,y]/(x^2,xy,y^2)$, and $k[x]/(x^5)$ are Artinian local with residue field $k$ — finite-dimensional over $k$ with a single maximal ideal that is nilpotent.
questions.explain: $k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$, $k[x,y]/(x^2,xy,y^2)$, and $k[x]/(x^5)$ are Artinian local with residue field $k$ — finite-dimensional over $k$ with a single maximal ideal that is nilpotent. $k[x]$ is not Artinian (infinite-dimensional, dim $1$). $k\times k$ is Artinian but not local — it has two maximal ideals.

questions.q: Which Artinian local $k$-algebra is the universal first-order thickening — the test ring for first-order deformations of $\mathrm{Spec}\,k$?
questions.hint: $k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ — the dual numbers — has length $2$ with $\mathfrak{m}^2 = 0$ and embedding dimension $1$.
questions.explain: $k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ — the dual numbers — has length $2$ with $\mathfrak{m}^2 = 0$ and embedding dimension $1$. A morphism $\mathrm{Spec}\,k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2) \to X_0$ is exactly a $k$-point together with a tangent vector; flat lifts of $X_0$ to $\mathrm{Spec}\,k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ are first-order deformations.

questions.q: Localizing $\mathbb{Z}$ at the prime $(p)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$. Its unique maximal ideal is:
questions.hint: A localization at a prime is a local ring; its maximal ideal is the extension of that prime.
questions.explain: A localization at a prime is a local ring; its maximal ideal is the extension of that prime. Elements of $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$ are fractions $a/b$ with $p\nmid b$, and the non-units are exactly the numerators divisible by $p$.

questions.q: How many primes less than $100$ survive in $\mathbb{Z}[1/2]$ (i.e. remain non-unit primes)?
questions.hint: Primes of $S^{-1}A$ correspond to primes of $A$ disjoint from $S$.
questions.explain: Primes of $S^{-1}A$ correspond to primes of $A$ disjoint from $S$. Inverting $2$ kills $(2)$, leaving odd primes $<100$: $3,5,7,11,\dots,97$, which is $24$ primes.

questions.q: The residue field $A_{\mathfrak{p}}/\mathfrak{p}A_{\mathfrak{p}}$ at a prime $\mathfrak{p}$ of $A$ equals:
questions.hint: Modding $A_{\mathfrak{p}}$ by its maximal ideal yields the fraction field of $A/\mathfrak{p}$.
questions.explain: Modding $A_{\mathfrak{p}}$ by its maximal ideal yields the fraction field of $A/\mathfrak{p}$. For $A=\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathfrak{p}=(p)$: residue field $\mathbb{F}_p=\mathrm{Frac}(\mathbb{Z}/p)$.

hard.q: Let $A=k[x,y]_{(x,y)}$ (local ring at the origin). Which ring is $A/(xy)$ as a local ring?
choices: $k[x,y]/(xy)$ localized at $(x,y)$, a non-domain local ring with two minimal primes
hard.explain: Localization and quotient commute: $A/(xy)=(k[x,y]/(xy))_{(x,y)}$ — the local ring of the node at the origin, non-domain with minimal primes $(x)$ and $(y)$.

hard.q: Let $A=\mathbb{Z}_{(5)}$. How many ideals does $A/(125)$ have?
hard.explain: $A/(125)\cong\mathbb{Z}/125$ as rings: local with chain $(0)\subsetneq(25)\subsetneq(5)\subsetneq(1)$. $4$ ideals.

hard.q: Arrange: why is localization exact? Pick the correct one-line proof.
choices: Surjectivity first, then injectivity via snake lemma
hard.explain: $S^{-1}M\cong M\otimes_A S^{-1}A$; $S^{-1}A$ is flat over $A$ (a direct-limit of free modules), so tensoring preserves exactness.

questions.q: Which $\mathbb{Z}$-module is NOT flat?
questions.hint: Over a PID, flat = torsion-free.
questions.explain: Over a PID, flat = torsion-free. $\mathbb{Z}/3$ has torsion.

questions.q: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2}\mathrm{Tor}_1^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$.
questions.hint: Resolve $\mathbb{Z}/2$ via $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$, tensor with $\mathbb{Z}/2$: $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, kernel $\mathbb{Z}/2$.
questions.explain: Resolve $\mathbb{Z}/2$ via $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$, tensor with $\mathbb{Z}/2$: $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, kernel $\mathbb{Z}/2$.

questions.q: For a Noetherian local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$, a finitely generated $A$-module $M$ is flat iff:
questions.hint: Over a Noetherian local ring, f.g.
questions.explain: Over a Noetherian local ring, f.g. flat = free (local criterion plus Nakayama).

hard.q: Let $0\to M'\to M\to M''\to 0$ be exact with $M''$ flat. Which is true?
hard.hint: Use the long exact Tor sequence.
hard.explain: $\mathrm{Tor}_1(M'',N)=0$ gives exactness of $0\to M'\otimes N\to M\otimes N$; extend by $\mathrm{Tor}_2$ for higher statements. So $M$ flat $\Leftrightarrow$ $M'$ flat.

hard.q: Compute the order of $\mathrm{Tor}_2^{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$.
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Z}$ has global dimension $1$, so $\mathrm{Tor}_i^{\mathbb{Z}}=0$ for $i\ge 2$. Trivial group has order $1$.

hard.q: Which example shows torsion-free $\ne$ flat over a non-PID?
hard.explain: $(x,y)$ is torsion-free (injects in fraction field), but not flat: $\mathrm{Tor}_1^{k[x,y]}(k[x,y]/(x,y),(x,y))\ne 0$.

nakayama-lemma-ca.title: Nakayama's lemma and Zariski tangent spaces

questions.q: Let $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ be local Noetherian, $M$ f.g. Elements $m_1,\dots,m_r\in M$ generate $M$ iff:
choices: their images span $M/\mathfrak{m}M$ as a $k$-vector space
questions.hint: Nakayama corollary: minimal generators biject with a basis of $M/\mathfrak{m}M$.
questions.explain: Nakayama corollary: minimal generators biject with a basis of $M/\mathfrak{m}M$.

questions.q: For the nodal cubic $y^2=x^3+x^2$ at the origin $p$, compute $\dim_k(\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)$ of the local ring at $p$.
questions.hint: Both partials of $y^2-x^3-x^2$ vanish at origin; Jacobian zero.
questions.explain: Both partials of $y^2-x^3-x^2$ vanish at origin; Jacobian zero. Zariski tangent space has dimension $2$ while the curve is $1$-dim: singular.

questions.q: A Noetherian local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ is regular when:
questions.hint: Regularity: embedding dimension = Krull dimension.
questions.explain: Regularity: embedding dimension = Krull dimension. $\dim_k\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2\ge\dim A$, equality iff regular.

hard.q: Pick the key step in proving $\mathfrak{m}N=N\Rightarrow N=0$ for $N\subseteq M$ a submodule of an f.g. module over a local ring.
choices: Cayley–Hamilton: produce $f(1)\cdot n = 0$ with $f(1)\in 1 + \mathfrak{m}$, a unit
hard.hint: Look for a monic polynomial whose constant term is a unit mod $\mathfrak{m}$.
hard.explain: $\mathfrak{m}N=N$ gives matrix equation $Mn=n$; Cayley–Hamilton yields $p(1)\in 1+\mathfrak{m}$ annihilating $N$. $p(1)$ is a unit, so $N=0$.

hard.q: Let $A=k[[t]]$, $\mathfrak{m}=(t)$. Compute $\dim_k\mathfrak{m}^3/\mathfrak{m}^4$.
hard.explain: $\mathfrak{m}^n=(t^n)$, so $\mathfrak{m}^3/\mathfrak{m}^4\cong k$ spanned by $t^3$.

hard.q: For a regular local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ of dim $d$, which is true of $\mathrm{gr}_\mathfrak{m}A$?
hard.explain: Regular $\Leftrightarrow$ $\mathrm{gr}_\mathfrak{m}A\cong k[x_1,\dots,x_d]$ (polynomial). Completion gives power series.

questions.q: Which is an integral extension?
questions.hint: $\sqrt{-5}$ satisfies $t^2+5=0$, monic over $\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.explain: $\sqrt{-5}$ satisfies $t^2+5=0$, monic over $\mathbb{Z}$.

questions.q: By Noether normalization, $B=k[x,y]/(xy-1)$ is finite over $k[z]$ for some $z$. Minimum $[B:k[z]]$?
questions.hint: Choose $z=x+y$: $x,y$ satisfy $t^2-zt+1=0$, so $B$ is free of rank $2$ over $k[z]$.
questions.explain: Choose $z=x+y$: $x,y$ satisfy $t^2-zt+1=0$, so $B$ is free of rank $2$ over $k[z]$.

questions.q: If $A\subseteq B$ is integral of domains, which fails in general?
choices: $A$ is a field iff $B$ is a field
questions.hint: $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ is integral over $\mathbb{Z}$ but not f.g.
questions.explain: $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ is integral over $\mathbb{Z}$ but not f.g.

hard.q: $A\subseteq B$ integral of Noetherian domains, $A$ integrally closed. Primes $\mathfrak{q}_1,\mathfrak{q}_2$ over $\mathfrak{p}$:
hard.explain: Galois group acts transitively on the fiber; in general fibers are incomparable but need not collapse.

hard.q: How many primes of $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ lie over $(5)\subset\mathbb{Z}$?
hard.explain: $5=(2+i)(2-i)$ splits since $5\equiv 1\pmod 4$. Two primes.

hard.q: In Noether normalization, the role of the linear change $x_i\mapsto x_i - \lambda_i x_n$ is to:
choices: make $x_n$ integral over $k[x_1,\dots,x_{n-1}]$ via a monic relation
hard.explain: Generic $\lambda$ ensures the top $x_n$-power coefficient in $f$ is nonzero; after scaling, monic in $x_n$, so $x_n$ is integral over the others.

questions.q: Krull dimension of $k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$?
questions.hint: $\dim k[x_1,\dots,x_n]=n$; chain $(0)\subsetneq(x_1)\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq(x_1,\dots,x_4)$.
questions.explain: $\dim k[x_1,\dots,x_n]=n$; chain $(0)\subsetneq(x_1)\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq(x_1,\dots,x_4)$.

questions.q: $\dim k[x,y]/(xy)$?
questions.hint: Two coordinate axes; longest chain is (generic of one axis) $\subsetneq$ (origin): dim $1$.
questions.explain: Two coordinate axes; longest chain is (generic of one axis) $\subsetneq$ (origin): dim $1$.

questions.q: $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]$?
questions.hint: $\dim A[x]=\dim A+1$ for Noetherian $A$.
questions.explain: $\dim A[x]=\dim A+1$ for Noetherian $A$. $\dim\mathbb{Z}=1$, so $\dim\mathbb{Z}[x]=2$. Chain: $(0)\subsetneq(p)\subsetneq(p,x)$.

hard.q: Compute $\dim k[x,y,z]/(xz-y^2, yz-x^3, z^2-x^2 y)$ (twisted cubic).
hard.explain: Twisted cubic is a $1$-dim curve $t\mapsto(t,t^2,t^3)$.

hard.q: Which is a Noetherian but non-catenary ring?
choices: Nagata's counterexample with inconsistent chain lengths
hard.explain: Nagata built a Noetherian domain with chains of two different maximal lengths. Polynomial rings, fields, and $\mathbb{Z}$ are universally catenary.

hard.q: Compute $\dim \mathbb{R}[x,y,z]/(x^2+y^2+z^2-1)$.
hard.explain: $2$-sphere is $2$-dim irreducible variety; $\dim$ = tr.deg = $2$.

questions.q: The transcendence degree $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k(k(x,y))$ of the field of rational functions in two variables is:
questions.hint: $\{x,y\}$ is algebraically independent over $k$, so $\mathrm{tr.deg}=2$.
questions.explain: $\{x,y\}$ is algebraically independent over $k$, so $\mathrm{tr.deg}=2$. This is the same as the Krull dimension of $k[x,y]$.

questions.q: Let $B = k[x,y,z]/(z - xy)$. Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$.
questions.hint: The relation $z=xy$ makes $z$ algebraically dependent on $x,y$.
questions.explain: The relation $z=xy$ makes $z$ algebraically dependent on $x,y$. $\mathrm{Frac}(B)=k(x,y)$, with tr.deg $2$. (Matches $\dim B = 2$.)

questions.q: For a finitely generated integral domain $B$ over an algebraically closed field $k$, which theorem asserts $\dim B = \mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B)$?
questions.hint: Noether normalization gives a finite map $B\twoheadleftarrow k[y_1,\dots,y_d]$ with $d=\mathrm{tr.deg}$; going-up preserves dimension; $\dim k[y_1,\dots,y_d]=d$.
questions.explain: Noether normalization gives a finite map $B\twoheadleftarrow k[y_1,\dots,y_d]$ with $d=\mathrm{tr.deg}$; going-up preserves dimension; $\dim k[y_1,\dots,y_d]=d$.

hard.q: Let $f\colon X\to Y$ be a dominant morphism of irreducible varieties over $\overline k$ with $\dim X > \dim Y$. Pick the correct generic-fiber statement.
choices: Every fiber of $f$ has dimension $\dim X - \dim Y$
The generic fiber has dimension $\dim X - \dim Y$; other fibers may jump
hard.explain: The dimension-of-fiber theorem: generic fiber has dim $=\dim X - \dim Y$; special fibers can only jump up. E.g. blow-up has generic fiber a point but exceptional fiber a curve.

hard.q: Compute $\mathrm{tr.deg}_{\mathbb{Q}}(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}, \pi, e))$ assuming $\pi, e$ are algebraically independent over $\mathbb{Q}$ (this is open, but assume it).
hard.explain: $\sqrt{2}$ is algebraic over $\mathbb{Q}$ (contributes $0$ to tr.deg). $\pi, e$ assumed algebraically independent contribute $2$. Total: $2$.

hard.q: For a purely transcendental extension $k\subset k(t_1,\dots,t_n)$, which is true?
choices: It has transcendence degree $n$ and is the fraction field of $k[t_1,\dots,t_n]$
hard.explain: Purely transcendental means a transcendence basis generates the extension. tr.deg = $n$; the field is $k(t_1,\dots,t_n) = \mathrm{Frac}(k[t_1,\dots,t_n])$.
